title: Choosing the Cell Resolution
text:
The scale-decomposition bound on preferential-sampling bias (Prop. scale-decomposition of the methodology) reads, on an equal-measure partition \(\mathcal{P}_K\) with \(K=K_{\mathrm{lon}}K_{\mathrm{lat}}K_t\), \[
  \big|B_{\mathrm{samp}}\big|
  \;\le\;\sigma_{w^{\star}}\,\sigma_{Y^{\star}}
        \;+\;\sigma_{w^{\perp}}\,\sigma_{Y^{\perp}} ,
\] with field factors \(\sigma_{Y^\star}=\|y^\star\|_{L^2(\nu)}\) (resolved, between-cell) and \(\sigma_{Y^\perp}=\|y^\perp\|_{L^2(\nu)}\) (within-cell), where \(y^\star=\mathsf{P}_K(y-\mu)\), \(y^\perp=\mathsf{P}_K^{\perp}(y-\mu)\) are the cell-averaging / within-cell projections of the OHC field \(y\in L^2(\nu)\) and \(\nu\) is the normalized area\(\times\)time reference measure on \(\mathcal{X}=A\times\mathcal{T}\). The one factor that cannot be estimated from a sparse point pattern, \(\sigma_{w^\perp}\), enters only through the product \(\sigma_{w^\perp}\sigma_{Y^\perp}\); it is therefore bias-irrelevant to the extent the field is constant within cells. So the cell scale must make the fraction of field variance below it, \(\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2/\sigma_Y^2\), small — while keeping the mean occupancy \(N/K\) (in practice \(F/K\), with \(F\) the number of distinct floats) above one.
The field factors, unlike \(\sigma_{w^\star}\), are computable directly on the dense GLORYS field over the NAC box (lon \([-68,-62]\), lat \([36,40]\), 2020–2022 daily at \(1/12^\circ\)), which resolves \(\sigma_{Y^\star},\sigma_{Y^\perp},\sigma_Y\) exactly. This report fixes the resolution in two logically separate steps — spatial (\(K_{\mathrm{lon}},K_{\mathrm{lat}}\)) and temporal (\(K_t\)) — and, importantly, the spatial choice can be made independently of seasonality (see below), so we settle it first.


Setup — run (or load cached) R analyses
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath("../code"))

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import resolution as R

DEPTHS = ["700", "2000"]
D = "../data/leakage"
TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV = f"{D}/leakage_time.csv", f"{D}/leakage_full.csv", f"{D}/leakage_spatial.csv"
VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF = f"{D}/variogram_ranges.csv", f"{D}/variogram_curves.csv", f"{D}/variogram_acf.csv"
ARGO_CSV, OCC_CSV, SWEEP_CSV = f"{D}/argo_compare.csv", f"{D}/argo_occupancy.csv", f"{D}/argo_sweep.csv"

if not all(os.path.exists(p) for p in (TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV)):
    R.run_leakage_curves(TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV, depths=DEPTHS)
if not all(os.path.exists(p) for p in (VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF)):
    R.run_variogram(VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF, depths=DEPTHS)
if not all(os.path.exists(p) for p in (ARGO_CSV, OCC_CSV, SWEEP_CSV)):
    R.run_argo_comparison(ARGO_CSV, OCC_CSV, SWEEP_CSV, depths=DEPTHS)

time_df, full_df, spatial_df = R.load_tables(TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV)
vg_ranges, vg_curves, vg_acf = R.load_tables(VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF)
argo_df, occ_df, sweep_df = R.load_tables(ARGO_CSV, OCC_CSV, SWEEP_CSV)

title: Choosing the Cell Resolution
text:
Decorrelation length (directional variogram)
The variogram corroborates the cell-size curve above and, unlike it, exposes the anisotropy that the front imposes. Within each snapshot we form grid-point pairs (subsampled), classify each by orientation, and accumulate the semivariance \(\tfrac12(y_i-y_j)^2\) into zonal (\(|\theta|<30^\circ\)), meridional (\(|\theta|>60^\circ\)) and omnidirectional bins, pooled over snapshots — again a per-snapshot construction, so again seasonality-free. It must be directional: correlation is long along the current and short across it, and an isotropic average would hide the short across-front scale, which is the binding one. (Raw OHC has a per-snapshot planar trend removed so the standing Gulf-Stream gradient does not render the variogram non-stationary; the deseasonalized panel removes the per-snapshot mean.)


0-700 m0-2000 m














The binding relevance scale is the shorter, meridional (across-front) range: \(\sigma_{Y^\perp}\) is controlled by field variation in every within-cell direction, so the shortest one governs. The methodology’s verdict is \(L_{\mathrm{rel}}\gtrsim s_F=\sqrt{|A|/F}\approx76\) km (eq. verdict): the field’s shortest decorrelation length must be no finer than the scale the floats can resolve. Where it holds, cells at the sampling scale sit within a decorrelation length and \(\sigma_{Y^\perp}\) is small; where it fails, a band of bias-relevant structure is unresolved and the residual term must be carried rather than assumed away.

title: Choosing the Cell Resolution
text:
Sampling weights (occupancy)
On the operating grid, occupancy estimates the cell sampling probability \(\pi_j = n_j/N\) and hence the between-cell weight \(\bar w_j = K\pi_j\) (\(\bar w_j = 1\) is uniform, \(\nu\)-matched sampling). Its dispersion is the resolved sampling factor \(\sigma_{w^\star}^2 = \tfrac1K\sum_j(\bar w_j-1)^2\) (eq. sigma-w-parts).


0-700 m





0-2000 m





The profiles keep all \(K_{\mathrm{lon}}\times K_{\mathrm{lat}}=30\) operating cells populated (\(\geq 5\) profiles each), with a moderate resolved dispersion \(\sigma_{w^\star}\approx 0.34\). Occupancy is the binding constraint on how fine the grid can go: as cells shrink past the float floor \(s_F\), cells empty out and the within-cell terms become inestimable.

title: Choosing the Cell Resolution
section: Within- and between-cell OHC: Argo vs GLORYS
text:
Within- and between-cell OHC: Argo vs GLORYS
For each \(K_t\) the profiles are binned into the same equal-measure \(\mathcal{P}_K\) cells; cell means and within-cell variances are taken over the profiles (unweighted — the sample already carries the sampling intensity), and the field factors use equal cell weight \(1/K\). Solid lines are the GLORYS truth (reference measure); open markers the Argo sample.


0-700 m





0-2000 m





At \(0\)–\(700\) m the sparse sample recovers the field structure well: the between-cell factor \(\sigma_{Y^\star}\) and the within-cell fraction \(\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2/\sigma_Y^2\) track the GLORYS truth closely, with the Argo \(\sigma_{Y^\perp}\) mildly inflated (few profiles per cell, and profiles in a cell span different days within the time bin add temporal spread to the “spatial” within-cell term). At \(0\)–\(2000\) m the Argo within-cell fraction is visibly inflated relative to truth — the variable profile-depth truncation flagged above, not a property of the field.

title: Choosing the Cell Resolution
section: Realized bias and the design-based cap
text:
Realized bias and the design-based cap
The naive profile average \(\hat\mu_\pi = \tfrac1N\sum_i y_i\) is the \(\pi\)-mean; its gap from the GLORYS \(\nu\)-mean \(\mu\) is the realized discrepancy, while the cell-averaged estimator \(\hat\mu_{\mathrm{grid}} = \tfrac1K\sum_j\bar y_j\) partially corrects the between-cell oversampling. The design-based between-cell cap \(\sigma_{w^\star}\sigma_{Y^\star}\) (observed \(\sigma_{w^\star}\), truth \(\sigma_{Y^\star}\)) bounds the preferential-sampling part of the bias.

At \(0\)–\(700\) m the naive profile mean underestimates the GLORYS truth by 2.74 GJ m\(^{-2}\) (\(-6.0\%\) of \(\mu=46.0\)), and cell-averaging trims this to 2.30. Even the tightest, pure-spatial cap (\(K_t=1\)) \(\sigma_{w^\star}\sigma_{Y^\star}=3.02\) GJ m\(^{-2}\) contains it — whereas at \(0\)–\(2000\) m the truncation-inflated gap exceeds that cap, confirming it is not sampling alone.







What this gap does and does not isolate. The realized naive/grid discrepancy conflates preferential sampling with any systematic GLORYS-vs-observation offset (reanalysis mean-state bias, representativeness) — and at \(0\)–\(2000\) m with the depth truncation above, which is why the \(2000\) m \(|\)bias\(|\) can exceed the preferential-sampling cap. Only the design-based product \(\sigma_{w^\star}\sigma_{Y^\star}\) (plus the within-cell term) isolates the sampling contribution as a worst case. The clean sampling-only bias — GLORYS co-located at the float positions, same field on both sides — is the subject of the main sampling-bias experiment; this section is an independent check with the real profiles that the sampling is preferential (\(\sigma_{w^\star}>0\)) and that the field structure the bound relies on is recoverable from the observed sample.

title: Targeted Analysis
text:
Goal and design
TODO


Setup
This report targets the same Gulf Stream / North Atlantic Current analysis region as pilot_simulation.qmd, but compares the GLORYS truth OHC field against the RG Argo Climatology across three years (2020, 2021, 2022) rather than just one. 2020 velocity and temperature come from data/velocity_gs_wide/ (lat 30–48\(^\circ\)N); 2021 and 2022 come from data/velocity_gs_2021_2022/ (lat 33–45\(^\circ\)N, downloaded by code/download_gs_2021_2022.py), a tighter latitude band over the same longitude span and GLORYS12V1 product. Both cover the analysis region with room to spare.


Setup — imports and experiment configuration
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath("../code"))

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import xarray as xr
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import ohc
import ohc_bias
import ohc_climatology as ohc_clim
from trajplots import map_fields_row

YEARS = [2020, 2021, 2022]

# --- per-year velocity/temperature source and its download domain ---
VELOCITY_GLOB = {
    2020: "../data/velocity_gs_wide/velocity_2020_*.nc",
    2021: "../data/velocity_gs_2021_2022/velocity_2021_*.nc",
    2022: "../data/velocity_gs_2021_2022/velocity_2022_*.nc",
}
DOWNLOAD_BOUNDS = {  # (lat0, lat1, lon0, lon1) -- as downloaded, differs by year
    2020: (30, 48, -74, -49),
    2021: (33, 45, -74, -49),
    2022: (33, 45, -74, -49),
}

ANALYSIS_BOUNDS = (36, 40, -68, -62)     # float deployment box (4$^\circ$ lat x 6$^\circ$ lon)
CONTEXT_BOUNDS  = (34, 42, -70, -60)     # ANALYSIS_BOUNDS + 2 deg margin, inside every year's domain
EXTENT_WIDE     = [-76, -47, 28, 50]     # cartopy map extent for the wide-context maps
DEPTHS          = ["ohc_700", "ohc_2000"]   # both depth layers, shown throughout




Analysis region
The analysis domain sits on the Gulf Stream / North Atlantic Current front — a sharp north–south OHC gradient. The annual-mean GLORYS OHC below shows high OHC (~90 GJ m\(^{-2}\)) to the south and low OHC (~45 GJ m\(^{-2}\)) to the north, for each of 2020, 2021, and 2022.
OHC definition. For every 1/12\(^\circ\) grid cell, OHC (GJ m\(^{-2}\)) is the integral \(\text{OHC} = \int_0^{z} \rho\, c_p\, T\, dz\) computed using the trapezoidal rule for \(z = 700\) m and \(z = 2000\) m where \(T\) is the sea water potential temperature in \(^\circ\)C reported at 50 standard depth levels (41 levels from 0m-2250m: 0.41m, 1.54m, …, 644m, 763m, …, 1942m, 2225m). Unlike Levitus et al. [2012], which computed \(\rho\) and \(c_p\) from annual climatological T/S fields, here both are fixed constants – adequate at this study’s scope (a \(4^\circ\times6^\circ\) region and a bias between two estimators computed the same way, so most of the T/S-dependence common to both cancels) but worth revisiting if the analysis later targets absolute OHC magnitudes or multi-year trends, where Levitus-style climatological \(\rho,c_p\) would matter more.


Load per-year GLORYS velocity/temperature and compute the true OHC field
# Cache truth_ohc_field (reads all 41 depth levels -- the dominant cost here)
# to NetCDF so re-rendering doesn't repeat it. Delete the .nc to force a recompute.
OHC_TRUTH_DIR = "../data/ohc_truth"
os.makedirs(OHC_TRUTH_DIR, exist_ok=True)

theta = {}
truth_field_full = {}
for y in YEARS:
    la0, la1, lo0, lo1 = DOWNLOAD_BOUNDS[y]
    theta[y] = xr.open_mfdataset(VELOCITY_GLOB[y]).sel(
        latitude=slice(la0, la1), longitude=slice(lo0, lo1),
    )
    cache_path = f"{OHC_TRUTH_DIR}/truth_ohc_{y}.nc"
    if os.path.exists(cache_path):
        truth_field_full[y] = xr.open_dataset(cache_path).load()
    else:
        truth_field_full[y] = ohc.truth_ohc_field(theta[y]).compute()
        truth_field_full[y].to_netcdf(cache_path)



202020212022




Code
_ = plot_ohc_field_year(2020)








Figure 1: GLORYS 1/12\(^\circ\) 2020-mean OHC (0–700 m and 0–2000 m)









Code
_ = plot_ohc_field_year(2021)








Figure 2: GLORYS 1/12\(^\circ\) 2021-mean OHC (0–700 m and 0–2000 m)









Code
_ = plot_ohc_field_year(2022)








Figure 3: GLORYS 1/12\(^\circ\) 2022-mean OHC (0–700 m and 0–2000 m)








Argo floats in this region park and drift at ~1000 m between profiling cycles. The annual-mean uo/vo near 1000 m below shows the strong North Atlantic / Gulf Stream current a float deployed here would be advected by, for each year.

202020212022




Code
_ = plot_velocity_1000_year(2020)








Figure 4: GLORYS 2020-mean horizontal velocity near the 1000 m parking depth.









Code
_ = plot_velocity_1000_year(2021)








Figure 5: GLORYS 2021-mean horizontal velocity near the 1000 m parking depth.









Code
_ = plot_velocity_1000_year(2022)








Figure 6: GLORYS 2022-mean horizontal velocity near the 1000 m parking depth.










OHC Anomaly
Climatology source. data/rg_climatology/RG_ArgoClim_Temperature_2019.nc (the RG Argo Climatology) contains ARGO_TEMPERATURE_MEAN: a single static mean temperature field (Jan 2004 – Dec 2018, no seasonal/monthly dimension) on a 1\(^\circ\) lat/lon grid and 58 pressure levels (2.5–1975 dbar). Its longitude is 0–360\(^\circ\)E and is converted to -180..180\(^\circ\) on load.
The twelve monthly extension files RG_ArgoClim_<YYYY><MM>_2019.nc each contain the observed ARGO_TEMPERATURE_ANOMALY from the 2004–2018 mean for each calendar month. We load these for each of 2020, 2021, and 2022.
Climatological OHC field. Either mean-temperature field is integrated over depth with the same trapezoidal rule used for GLORYS (ohc.profile_ohc, treating RG’s pressure in dbar as meters, the same approximation already used throughout this report), giving an ohc_700/ohc_2000 field on RG’s native 1\(^\circ\) grid.
Linear interpolation from 1\(^\circ\) to 1/12\(^\circ\). Because the true GLORYS temperature field is on a 1/12\(^\circ\) grid, code/ohc_climatology.py bilinearly interpolates the climatological OHC to the 1/12\(^\circ\) grid for each calendar month.
Anomaly. For a grid cell at \(x = (x_{lat}, x_{lon})\) observed in month \(t\) of year \(y\), the OHC anomaly is calculated by subtracting the spatially interpolated climatological OHC for that calendar month (built from that year’s RG extension file) from the observed monthly-mean GLORYS OHC value. This is repeated independently for 2020, 2021, and 2022, each against its own year’s RG climatology anomaly.


Load the RG climatology (seasonal, per year + static) and compute the truth OHC anomaly for 2020-2022
mean_da = ohc_clim.load_rg_mean()
clim_field_B = ohc_clim.climatological_ohc_field(mean_da)  # static 2004-2018 mean

clim_field = {}       # per-year seasonal climatology (Option A), month dim 1-12
truth_field = {}      # per-year truth field restricted to the analysis region
truth_anom = {}       # per-year GLORYS truth OHC anomaly (native 1/12$^\circ$)
truth_anom_cells = {}
for y in YEARS:
    ext_da = ohc_clim.load_rg_extensions(year=y)
    clim_field[y] = ohc_clim.climatological_ohc_field(mean_da + ext_da).compute()
    truth_field[y] = truth_field_full[y].sel(
        latitude=slice(ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[0], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[1]),
        longitude=slice(ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[2], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[3]),
    )
    truth_anom[y] = ohc_clim.truth_ohc_anomaly(truth_field[y], clim_field[y])
    truth_anom_cells[y] = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(truth_anom[y])

# truth_field[y]'s spatial grid is identical across years (same ANALYSIS_BOUNDS
# slice of the same native 1/12 deg grid) -- only the time axis differs -- so
# concatenating along time lets downstream monthly-mean/coarsen calls run once
# on all three years instead of once per year.
truth_field_all = xr.concat([truth_field[y] for y in YEARS], dim="time")

# All three years concatenated -- one 36-month table for the multi-year maps/limits below.
truth_anom_cells_all = pd.concat(truth_anom_cells.values(), ignore_index=True)

# Publish both to data/ so nac_gp.qmd (and any other downstream report) can load
# them directly instead of re-deriving them from the raw RG climatology files.
truth_field_all.to_netcdf(f"{OHC_TRUTH_DIR}/truth_field_analysis.nc")
truth_anom_cells_all.to_csv(f"{OHC_TRUTH_DIR}/truth_anom_cells_all.csv", index=False)

# Shared diverging colour range for every *anomaly* map below, across all years.
ANOM_VLIM = {d: float(truth_anom_cells_all[d].abs().quantile(0.98)) * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ for d in DEPTHS}


Below is comparison of the RG climatology published temperature trend and my recreation



Published RG Climatology global-mean temperature time series (source: sio-argo.ucsd.edu)




Reconstruct published RG global-mean temperature time series (2004–2026)
import matplotlib.dates as mdates
from pathlib import Path

_ds_rg  = xr.open_dataset(ohc_clim.RG_MEAN_PATH, decode_times=False)
_mean_v = _ds_rg["ARGO_TEMPERATURE_MEAN"].values    # (pres, lat, lon)
_anom_v = _ds_rg["ARGO_TEMPERATURE_ANOMALY"].values # (180, pres, lat, lon)
_p      = _ds_rg["PRESSURE"].values
_lat    = _ds_rg["LATITUDE"].values
_ds_rg.close()

_dp     = np.diff(_p)
_coslat = np.cos(np.deg2rad(_lat))

def _vol_mean_T(T):
    T_mid = (T[:-1] + T[1:]) / 2    # trapezoidal averages; NaN where either endpoint NaN
    w     = _dp[:, None, None] * _coslat[None, :, None]
    return float(np.nansum(T_mid * w) / np.nansum(np.where(np.isnan(T_mid), 0.0, w)))

_dates_main = pd.date_range("2004-01", periods=180, freq="MS")
_tmean_main = np.array([_vol_mean_T(_mean_v + _anom_v[i]) for i in range(180)])

# Load all available extension months (2019 onward)
_ext_targets = [(y, m) for y in range(2019, 2027) for m in range(1, 13)
                if not (y == 2026 and m > 2)]
_ext_dates, _ext_vals = [], []
for _y, _m in _ext_targets:
    _path = f"../data/rg_climatology/RG_ArgoClim_{_y}{_m:02d}_2019.nc"
    if not Path(_path).exists():
        continue
    _ds = xr.open_dataset(_path, decode_times=False)
    _anom = _ds["ARGO_TEMPERATURE_ANOMALY"].values.squeeze(0)
    _ext_vals.append(_vol_mean_T(_mean_v + _anom))
    _ext_dates.append(pd.Timestamp(_y, _m, 1))
    _ds.close()

# Bridge the Dec 2018 / Jan 2019 gap by prepending the last climatology point
# to the extension series so the two line segments share an endpoint.
_bridge_date = pd.Timestamp(2018, 12, 1)
_bridge_val  = _tmean_main[-1]

_all_dates = np.array([_bridge_date] + _ext_dates, dtype="datetime64[ns]")
_all_vals  = np.concatenate([[_bridge_val], _ext_vals])

_smooth  = pd.Series(
    np.concatenate([_tmean_main, _ext_vals]),
).rolling(12, center=True, min_periods=12).mean().values
_sm_dates = np.array(list(_dates_main) + _ext_dates, dtype="datetime64[ns]")
_sm_ok    = ~np.isnan(_smooth)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4))
ax.plot(_dates_main, _tmean_main, color="black", lw=0.8, label="2004–2018 (mean + anom)")
ax.plot(_all_dates, _all_vals, color="red", lw=0.8,
        label=f"2019–{_ext_dates[-1].year} extensions")
ax.plot(_sm_dates[_sm_ok], _smooth[_sm_ok], color="limegreen", lw=1,
        label="12-month running mean")
for y in YEARS:
    ax.axvspan(pd.Timestamp(y, 1, 1), pd.Timestamp(y, 12, 31), color="#4a90d9", alpha=0.06)
ax.set_ylabel("Potential Temperature ($^\circ$C)")
ax.set_xlim(_dates_main[0], _all_dates[-1])
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.YearLocator(2))
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter("%Y"))
ax.grid(alpha=0.2)
fig.tight_layout()








Figure 7: Verification: global-mean RG potential temperature reconstructed from mean + monthly extension anomalies. The ~0.04 \(^\circ\)C upward offset vs the published plot above is expected — the RG website excludes marginal seas and the Arctic from its spatial average; we include all non-NaN cells.







RG climatology (static), native 1\(^\circ\) grid vs interpolated to 1/12\(^\circ\)
_clim_B_interp = ohc_clim.climatology_on_grid(
    clim_field_B, truth_field_full[2020]["latitude"].values, truth_field_full[2020]["longitude"].values
)

_STATIC_MAP_KW = dict(
    titles=["ohc 700m", "ohc 2000m"],
    cmaps=["viridis", "viridis"], cbar_labels=["GJ m$^{{-2}}$", "GJ m$^{{-2}}$"],
    extent=EXTENT_WIDE,
    boxes=[(*DOWNLOAD_BOUNDS[2020], "download domain", '0.5', "-"),
           (*ANALYSIS_BOUNDS, "analysis domain")],
)

_ = map_fields_row(
    [clim_field_B["ohc_700"] * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ, clim_field_B["ohc_2000"] * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ],
    suptitle="RG climatology (native 1$^\circ$)", **_STATIC_MAP_KW,
)
_ = map_fields_row(
    [_clim_B_interp["ohc_700"] * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ, _clim_B_interp["ohc_2000"] * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ],
    suptitle="RG climatology (bilinear -> 1/12$^\circ$)", **_STATIC_MAP_KW,
)











(a)











(b)






Figure 8





RG climatology by year and month, native 1\(^\circ\) grid vs interpolated to 1/12\(^\circ\), the GLORYS truth, the GLORYS truth - RG climatology anomaly, and eastward/northward velocity
# Fixed view for every panel below, regardless of its native grid resolution.
ANALYSIS_XLIM = (ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[2], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[3])
ANALYSIS_YLIM = (ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[0], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[1])

_clim_A_plot_years, _clim_A_interp_cells_years = [], []
for y in YEARS:
    months_y = [pd.Timestamp(y, m, 1) for m in clim_field[y]["month"].values]
    plot_y = clim_field[y].sel(
        latitude=slice(CONTEXT_BOUNDS[0], CONTEXT_BOUNDS[1]),
        longitude=slice(CONTEXT_BOUNDS[2], CONTEXT_BOUNDS[3]),
    ).assign_coords(month=months_y)
    _clim_A_plot_years.append(plot_y)
    _clim_A_interp_cells_years.append(
        ohc_clim.climatology_cells_table(
            clim_field[y], truth_field_full[2020]["latitude"].values, truth_field_full[2020]["longitude"].values,
            months=months_y,
        )
    )

# One 36-month table per map kind, across all three years.
_clim_A_native_cells = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(xr.concat(_clim_A_plot_years, dim="month"))
_clim_A_interp_cells = pd.concat(_clim_A_interp_cells_years, ignore_index=True)

# GLORYS truth, monthly-mean, same grid as the anomaly. truth_field_all already
# carries real per-year dates, so one resample over all three years gives
# correctly-dated months directly -- no per-year loop/relabeling needed.
truth_monthly_all = truth_field_all.resample(time="1MS").mean().rename({"time": "month"})
truth_monthly_cells_all = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(truth_monthly_all)

# Velocity near the 1000 m parking depth, monthly-mean, analysis region only.
# theta[y] itself can't be concatenated (different years cover different lat/lon
# extents -- see DOWNLOAD_BOUNDS), but restricting each to ANALYSIS_BOUNDS first
# gives identical grids that can be, so the resample below still runs once.
_theta_analysis_all = xr.concat(
    [
        theta[y][["uo", "vo"]].sel(depth=1000, method="nearest").sel(
            latitude=slice(ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[0], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[1]),
            longitude=slice(ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[2], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[3]),
        )
        for y in YEARS
    ],
    dim="time",
)
velocity_monthly_all = _theta_analysis_all.resample(time="1MS").mean().compute().rename({"time": "month"})
velocity_cells_all = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(velocity_monthly_all, value_cols=("uo", "vo"))
VEL_VLIM = float(pd.concat([velocity_cells_all["uo"], velocity_cells_all["vo"]]).abs().quantile(0.98))

# Shared absolute-OHC colour range across native/interpolated climatology and truth.
_abs_bounds = {
    d: (float(pd.concat([_clim_A_native_cells[d], truth_monthly_cells_all[d]]).mul(ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ).quantile(0.02)),
        float(pd.concat([_clim_A_native_cells[d], truth_monthly_cells_all[d]]).mul(ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ).quantile(0.98)))
    for d in DEPTHS
}

def show_clim_map(kind, d):
    cmin, cmax = _abs_bounds[d]
    common = dict(width=TABSET_FIGW, ncols=6, xlim=ANALYSIS_XLIM, ylim=ANALYSIS_YLIM)
    if kind == "native":
        return ohc_bias.plot_monthly_cell_maps(
            _clim_A_native_cells, d, vmin=cmin, vmax=cmax,
            title=f"RG climatology {d} by year/month (native 1$^\circ$)", **common)
    if kind == "interp":
        return ohc_bias.plot_monthly_cell_maps(
            _clim_A_interp_cells, d, vmin=cmin, vmax=cmax,
            title=f"RG climatology {d} by year/month (bilinear -> 1/12$^\circ$)", **common)
    if kind == "truth":
        return ohc_bias.plot_monthly_cell_maps(
            truth_monthly_cells_all, d, vmin=cmin, vmax=cmax,
            title=f"GLORYS truth {d} by year/month (native 1/12$^\circ$)", **common)
    if kind == "anom":
        return ohc_bias.plot_monthly_cell_maps(
            truth_anom_cells_all, d, vmin=-ANOM_VLIM[d], vmax=ANOM_VLIM[d], cmap="RdBu_r",
            cbar_label=f"{d} anomaly (GJ m$^{{-2}}$)",
            title=f"GLORYS truth - RG climatology (native 1/12$^\circ$)", **common)

def show_velocity_map(component):
    label = "eastward" if component == "uo" else "northward"
    return ohc_bias.plot_monthly_cell_maps(
        velocity_cells_all, component, vmin=-VEL_VLIM, vmax=VEL_VLIM, cmap="RdBu_r",
        value_scale=1, cbar_label=f"{component} (m s$^{{-1}}$)",
        title=f"GLORYS {label} velocity ({component}) ~1000 m, by year/month",
        width=TABSET_FIGW, ncols=6, xlim=ANALYSIS_XLIM, ylim=ANALYSIS_YLIM,
    )

_emit("::: {.panel-tabset}")
for d in DEPTHS:
    _emit(f"## {DEPTH_LABEL[d]}")
    _emit("::: {.panel-tabset}")
    for kind, label in [("native", "Native 1$^\circ$"), ("interp", "Interpolated to 1/12$^\circ$"),
                         ("truth", "GLORYS truth"), ("anom", "Anomaly")]:
        _emit(f"### {label}")
        _show_fig(show_clim_map(kind, d))
    _emit(":::")
_emit("## Eastward velocity")
_show_fig(show_velocity_map("uo"))
_emit("## Northward velocity")
_show_fig(show_velocity_map("vo"))
_emit(":::")

Code
def _box_mean(field, bounds=ANALYSIS_BOUNDS, value_cols=DEPTHS):
    la0, la1, lo0, lo1 = bounds
    sub = field.sel(latitude=slice(la0, la1), longitude=slice(lo0, lo1))
    w = np.cos(np.deg2rad(sub["latitude"]))
    return sub[list(value_cols)].weighted(w).mean(dim=("latitude", "longitude"))

box_by_year = {y: _box_mean(clim_field[y]) for y in YEARS}  # dims: month (1-12), per year
box_B = _box_mean(clim_field_B)  # scalar, static 2004-2018 reference

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 4), sharex=True)
months = np.arange(1, 13)
palette = plt.get_cmap("tab10").colors
for ax, d in zip(axes, DEPTHS):
    for i, y in enumerate(YEARS):
        ax.plot(months, box_by_year[y][d] * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ, "-o", color=palette[i], label=f"{y} mean")
    ax.axhline(float(box_B[d]) * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ, color="0.4", ls="--", label="static 2004-2018")
    ax.set_title(f"{d} background OHC, analysis-region mean")
    ax.set_xlabel("calendar month")
    ax.set_xticks(months)
    ax.grid(alpha=0.2)
axes[0].set_ylabel("OHC (GJ m$^{-2}$)")
axes[0].legend(frameon=False, fontsize="small")
fig.tight_layout()








Figure 10: RG climatological OHC, area-mean over the analysis region, by year

title: Preferential-Sampling Bounds
Preferential-Sampling Bounds
Preferential-Sampling Bounds
text:
\(L^2\) Worst-Case Bound
Assume
\[\begin{gather*}
Y,\;w-1\in L^2(\nu),
\end{gather*}\]
and write \(\sigma_Y = \|Y-\mu\|_2\) for the space-time standard deviation of the field and \(\sigma_w =
\|w-1\|_2\) for the coefficient of variation of the sampling intensity, i.e., how inhomogeneous the sampling is.
Applying the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality in \(L^2(\nu)\) to the covariance gives a bound:
\[\begin{gather*}
\boxed{\; |B_{\mathrm{samp}}| \;=\; |\operatorname{Cov}(w,Y)| \;=\; |\rho_{w,Y}|\,\sigma_w\,\sigma_Y \;\le\; \sigma_w\,\sigma_Y . \;}
\end{gather*}\]
This bound requires only finite second moments and imposes no smoothness assumptions on the underlying field.
Neither \(\sigma_Y\) nor \(\sigma_w\) is directly observable therefore we need an estimation strategy.

Estimating \(\sigma_Y\) and \(\sigma_w\)
The deterministic bound depends on the variability of both the field and the sampling design. In practice, these quantities must be estimated.
The field variability, \(\sigma_Y^2 = \int_{\mathcal X} (Y-\mu)^2\,d\nu\), may be estimated from:

A complete reanalysis product: Treat the gridded reanalysis, such as GLORYS12, as a proxy for the true field and compute its area-weighted spatial variance.
A Gaussian process (GP) model: Use the posterior mean to estimate the spatial variance, optionally incorporating posterior predictive uncertainty.
Observed float measurements: Compute the sample variance of the observed OHC values. This estimates variability under the sampling distribution rather than the target distribution and may therefore be biased under preferential sampling.

The sampling variability, \(\sigma_w^2 = \int_{\mathcal X}(w-1)^2\,d\nu\), depends only on the sampling intensity and can be estimated using the same approaches later employed to compute the RKHS norm of the representer. Since the sampling density \(w\) is unknown, it must first be estimated from the observed float locations.
Possible approaches include

kernel density estimation (KDE),
Gaussian process intensity models,
log-Gaussian Cox processes, or
other smooth spatial or spatio-temporal point process models.

Given an estimated intensity \(\hat w\), the integral may be approximated numerically using quadrature or Monte Carlo integration.


Illustrative Example
Consider an idealized sampling design in which floats sample uniformly over a space-time subregion \(D_f \subseteq \mathcal X\) and never sample outside it. Let \(f=\nu(D_f)\) denote the fraction of the domain covered by the sampling design. The corresponding sampling probability measure is \(\pi(E)=\frac{\nu(E\cap D_f)}{f}\), whose density with respect to \(\nu\) is
In this case, \(\sigma_w^2=\frac{1-f}{f}\), so the general Cauchy–Schwarz bound reduces to
\[\begin{gather*}
\big|B_{\mathrm{samp}}\big|
\le
\sigma_Y\sqrt{\frac{1-f}{f}}.
\end{gather*}\]






NoteDerivation





Since \(w=1/f\) on \(D_f\) (whose measure is \(f\)) and \(w=0\) on its complement (whose measure is \(1-f\)),
\[\begin{align*}
\sigma_w^2 &= \int_{\mathcal X}(w-1)^2\,d\nu \\
&= f\left(\frac1f-1\right)^2 + (1-f)(0-1)^2 \\
&= \frac{(1-f)^2}{f} + (1-f) = (1-f)\left(\frac{1-f}{f}+1\right) = \frac{1-f}{f}.
\end{align*}\]
Hence, \(|B_{\mathrm{samp}}| \le \sigma_Y\sqrt{\frac{1-f}{f}}.\)



The amplification factor \(\sqrt{\frac{1-f}{f}}\) depends only on the fraction of the domain sampled. It grows without bound as \(f\to0\), reflecting the increasing potential for sampling bias under sparse coverage, and goes to \(0\) as \(f\to1\), corresponding to complete coverage of the domain.


Code
f = np.linspace(0.01, 1.0, 400)
amp = np.sqrt((1 - f) / f)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.2))
ax.plot(f, amp, color="#1f77b4", lw=2)
ax.set_xlabel("covered space-time fraction  f")
ax.set_ylabel(r"amplification  $\sqrt{(1-f)/f}$")
ax.set_ylim(0, 6)
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)
_show_fig(fig)








Figure 1: Illustrative amplification factor sqrt((1-f)/f) vs the covered space-time fraction f, under an illustrative intensity.

title: Preferential-Sampling Bounds
text:
RKHS Worst-Case Bound
The Cauchy–Schwarz cap \(\sigma_w\sigma_Y\) is distribution-free: it assumes nothing about the field beyond a finite variance. Suppose instead the field is smooth, such that it lives in the RKHS \(\mathcal{H}_k\) of a covariance kernel \(k\).
Conditional on the sampling design (so that \(w\), and hence \(\pi\), are fixed), the sampling bias is a bounded linear functional of the field
\[\begin{gather*}
B_{\mathrm{samp}} \;=\; L(Y) ;=\; \int_{\mathcal X} Y(x)\,d\pi(x) - \int_{\mathcal X} Y(x)\,d\nu(x) .
\end{gather*}\]
Applying the reproducing property \(Y(x) = \langle Y,k(\cdot,x)\rangle_{\mathcal H_k}\) (Muandet et al. 2017),
\[\begin{align*}
L(Y) &= \int \left\langle Y,\, k(\cdot,x) \right\rangle \,d\pi(x) - \int \left\langle Y,\, k(\cdot,x) \right\rangle \,d\nu(x) \\
&= \left\langle Y,\ \int k(\cdot,x)\,d\pi(x)\right\rangle - \left\langle Y,\ \int k(\cdot,x)\,d\nu(x)\right\rangle \\
&= \left\langle Y,\, \xi_{\pi} \right\rangle - \left\langle Y,\, \xi_{\nu} \right\rangle \;=\; \left\langle Y,\, \xi_{\pi} - \xi_{\nu} \right\rangle ,
\end{align*}\]
where \(\xi_\pi := \int_{\mathcal X} k(\cdot,x)\,d\pi(x)\) and \(\xi_\nu := \int_{\mathcal X}
k(\cdot,x)\,d\nu(x)\) are the kernel mean embeddings of \(\pi\) and \(\nu\) into \(\mathcal H_k\).
Let \(\psi := \xi_\pi - \xi_\nu\), this is exactly \(L(Y) = \langle
Y,\psi\rangle_{\mathcal H_k}\). By the Riesz Representation Theorem, \(\psi\) is the unique element of \(\mathcal H_k\) representing \(L\).
This \(\psi\) is the discrepancy, in the RKHS, between where the floats actually sampled and the uniform target measure. Namely, \(\psi\) is zero when \(\pi=\nu\) (non-preferential sampling) and its norm \(\|\psi\|_{\mathcal H_k} = \|\xi_\pi - \xi_\nu\|_{\mathcal H_k}\) is the (kernel) maximum mean discrepancy between \(\pi\) and \(\nu\).
Applying the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality in the RKHS,
\[\begin{gather*}
|B_{\mathrm{samp}}| = |\langle Y,\psi\rangle_{\mathcal H_k}| \le \|Y\|_{\mathcal H_k}\,\|\psi\|_{\mathcal H_k},
\end{gather*}\]
so if \(\|Y\|_{\mathcal H_k}\le R\), maximizing over the smoothness ball gives:
\[\begin{gather*}
\boxed{\; \sup_{\|Y\|_{\mathcal H_k} \le R} |B_{\mathrm{samp}}| \;=\; R\,\|\psi\|_{\mathcal H_k}, \;}
\end{gather*}\]
attained at the worst-case field \(Y^\star = R\psi/\|\psi\|_{\mathcal H_k}\).
Compared with the \(L^2\) bound, the RKHS bound exploits the smoothness encoded by the kernel; since the admissible class of functions is substantially smaller than all of \(L^2(\nu)\), this bound is typically much tighter.

title: Preferential-Sampling Bounds
section: Computing the RKHS Norm of the Representer

title: Preferential-Sampling Bounds
text:
Estimating the RKHS Radius
The remaining quantity,
\[\begin{gather*}
R=\|Y\|_{\mathcal H_k},
\end{gather*}\]
measures the complexity of the unknown true field relative to the chosen kernel.
Possible estimation strategies include
1. Plug-in estimate from the fitted Gaussian process. Let \(\hat Y\) denote the posterior mean from a fitted Gaussian process model. The RKHS norm \(\|\hat Y\|_{\mathcal H_k}\) provides a natural measure of the complexity of the reconstructed field. Because GP regression regularizes the estimate, this quantity should be viewed as a proxy (or approximate lower bound) for the unknown RKHS norm of the true field. For noiseless kernel interpolation,
\[\begin{gather*}
\hat Y(x) = \sum_{i=1}^n \alpha_i k(x_i,x), \qquad \alpha=K^{-1}y,
\end{gather*}\]
and
\[\begin{gather*}
\|\hat Y\|_{\mathcal H_k}^2 = y^\top K^{-1}y.
\end{gather*}\]
Where \(K_{ij} = k(x_i,x_j)\), the GP covariance matrix.
Since this interpolant is the minimum-RKHS-norm function matching the data,
\[\begin{gather*}
\|\hat Y\|_{\mathcal H_k} \le \|Y\|_{\mathcal H_k}.
\end{gather*}\]
In Gaussian process regression (kriging), the posterior mean generally does not exactly interpolate the observations because of measurement error or nugget effects. Consequently, the quadratic form above is replaced by the corresponding expression involving the covariance matrix and observation error covariance (e.g., \((K+\Sigma_\varepsilon)^{-1}\)). The interpretation remains the same: the fitted GP norm serves as a regularized measure of field complexity.
2. Sensitivity analysis. Since \(R\) cannot be identified from the observations alone, an alternative is to report
\[\begin{gather*}
|B_{\mathrm{samp}}| \le R\,\|\hat\psi\|_{\mathcal H_k}
\end{gather*}\]
over a range of plausible values of \(R\). This separates uncertainty due to the unknown smoothness of the field from uncertainty due to the sampling design.

title: Preferential-Sampling Bounds
text:
Summary
Both bounds are Cauchy–Schwarz applied to the same covariance \(\int (w-1)(Y-\mu)\,d\nu\), but in different inner-product spaces – and that changes which fields the worst case ranges over.

In \(L^2(\nu)\) (the bound \(\sigma_w\sigma_Y\)): the worst-case field is \(Y-\mu \propto w-1\). Distribution-free, but pessimistic.
In \(\mathcal{H}_k\) (the bound \(R\|\psi\|\)): the worst-case field is \(Y^\star \propto \psi\). Requires additional assumptions, but gives tighter bound.

title: Argo Bias
text: This site collects analysis reports for a study of ocean heat content sampling bias in Argo float observations.

title: Argo Bias
text: This site collects analysis reports for a study of ocean heat content sampling bias in Argo float observations.

title: Setup and Bias Decomposition
text:
Setup
Argo floats sample the ocean sparsely and non-randomly. In a region with a strong current and a sharp heat-content gradient, e.g., a region in the North Atlantic Current \(A = [36^\circ,40^\circ]\,\mathrm{N}
\times [-68^\circ,-62^\circ]\,\mathrm{E}\), floats are advected along the flow and may preferentially occupy waters whose ocean heat content (OHC) differs systematically from the regional average. Any estimate of regional-mean OHC built by interpolating float observations therefore inherits a sampling-induced bias. This report explores methods to characterize that bias.
Let \(x = (s,t) \in \mathcal{X}\) be a generic space-time point for a spatial location \(s \in A \subset S^2\), the observation region, and time \(t \in \mathcal{T} \subset \mathbb{R}\), the observation period.
Let \(Y(x)\) be the true OHC field over region \(A\) and study period \(\mathcal{T}\), a continuous field on the space-time domain \(\mathcal{X} := A \times \mathcal{T}\).
Let \(|\cdot|\) denote surface (area) measure on \((A, \mathcal{B}(A))\) Define \(\alpha\) as the induced normalized area probability measure, \(\alpha(E) = |E|/|A|\) for \(E \in \mathcal{B}(A)\). And let \(\tau\) be a normalized probability measure on \(\mathcal{T}\).
Define \(\nu := \alpha\otimes\tau\) as the resulting space-time probability measure on the probability space \((\mathcal{X},\; \mathcal{B}(A)\otimes\mathcal{B}(\mathcal{T})\).
Our target estimand is the space-time mean OHC:
\[\begin{gather*}
\mu \;=\; \int_{\mathcal{X}} Y \,d\nu \;=\; \mathbb{E}[Y] .
\end{gather*}\]


Bias decomposition
The floats realize a spatio-temporal point process \(N\) on \((\mathcal{X},\, \mathcal{B}(A)\otimes\mathcal{B}(\mathcal{T}))\) with intensity measure \(\Lambda(E) = \mathbb{E}_N[N(E)]\), the expected number of profiles falling in the measureable set \(E\).
Assume \(\Lambda \ll \nu\), where \(\nu\) denotes the reference space-time volume measure.
Normalizing the intensity yields the sampling probability measure \(\pi \;=\; \frac{\Lambda}{\Lambda(\mathcal{X})}\), whose density with respect to \(\nu\) is the sampling weight \(w \;=\; \frac{d\pi}{d\nu}\), so that \(\pi(E) = \int_{E} w\,d\nu\), for every measurable set \(E\). Formally, \(w\) is the Radon–Nikodym derivative of \(\pi\) with respect to \(\nu\).






TipIntuition




\(\nu\) is the target population distribution (uniform over space-time)
\(\pi\) is the sampling distribution. If you draw a profile at random from the sampling process, \(\pi(E)\) is the probability that the profile lies in the space-time region \(E\).
\(w\) is the relative sampling propensity.

\(w(x) = 1\) floats are sampled as if uniformly near \(x\)
\(w(x) = 2\) floats are sampled twice as often compared to uniform sampling near \(x\)
\(w(x) = 0\) floats are never sampled near \(x\)


Under non-preferential sampling, \(w \equiv 1\), such that \(\pi = \nu\)


The design-mean is the field integrated against the sampling measure,
\[\begin{gather*}
\mu_w \qquad = \int_{\mathcal X} Y\,d\pi = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[Y] \qquad = \int_{\mathcal X} w\,Y\,d\nu = \mathbb{E}_{\nu}[w\,Y].
\end{gather*}\]
We can then decompose the bias for any estimator \(\hat \mu\) based on the observed \(Y(x)\):
\[\begin{gather*}
B \;=\; \hat\mu - \mu \;=\; \underbrace{\big(\mu_w - \mu\big)}_{\displaystyle B_{\mathrm{samp}}}
\;+\; \underbrace{\big(\hat\mu - \mu_w\big)}_{\displaystyle B_{\mathrm{pred}}} .
\end{gather*}\]

\(B_{\mathrm{samp}}\) preferential-sampling bias. Bias from floats preferential sampling of the OHC field.
\(B_{\mathrm{pred}}\) prediction bias. Bias from interpolation/kriging.

Critically, we have the sampling bias covariance identity:
\[\begin{gather*}
\boxed{\;B_{\mathrm{samp}} = \mu_w - \mu = \mathbb{E}_{\nu}[w\,Y] - \mathbb{E}_{\nu}[Y] \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\nu}[(w-1)\,Y] \;=\; \operatorname{Cov}_{\nu}(w,\, Y).\;}
\end{gather*}\]
Because, \(\operatorname{Cov}(w,Y) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} (w-1)(Y-\mu)\,d\nu = \int_{\mathcal X} (w - 1)Y\,d\nu = \mathbb{E}_{\nu}[(w-1)Y]\) since \(\mu\int_{\mathcal{X}} (w-1)\,d\nu = \mu(\mathbb{E}_{\nu}[w] - 1) = 0\).






NoteResult



Therefore, if there is no correlation between the sampling intensity and the OHC field, i.e., \(\operatorname{Cov}_{\nu}(w,Y) = 0\), then \(B_{\mathrm{samp}} = 0\).

title: Monte Carlo Null Distribution
text:
The construction below generates a Monte Carlo reference (null) distribution for preferential-sampling bias under the hypothesis that sampling is independent of OHC after accounting for the physical sampling constraints. The resulting null distribution can be used to assess whether the observed bias is larger than expected under non-preferential sampling and to gauge how conservative the deterministic worst-case bounds are.
Consider the null hypothesis that floats exhibit no preference for warm or cold OHC regions, so that the sampling design is independent of the OHC field after conditioning on the background sampling process.
Constrained-null resampling. Let \(\lambda_0\) denote an estimated non-preferential sampling intensity on \(\mathcal X\). In practice, \(\lambda_0\) might be obtained from a smooth intensity model fitted to the observed float locations with the OHC field excluded, or from a mechanistic deployment-and-advection model. Normalizing gives the probability measure \(\pi_0 \propto \lambda_0\).
Because the true field \(Y\) and its domain mean \(\mu = \int_{\mathcal X} Y\,d\nu\) are unknown, the procedure is carried out using an estimated field \(\hat Y\) (e.g., a reanalysis product). For \(b=1,\ldots,B\), draw an i.i.d. sample \(\{x_i^{(b)}\}_{i=1}^n\) from \(\pi_0\), where each \(x_i^{(b)}=(s_i^{(b)},t_i^{(b)})\), and compute
\[\begin{gather*}
\hat B_{\mathrm{samp}}^{(b)}
=
\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n
\hat Y\!\left(x_i^{(b)}\right)
-
\hat\mu,
\qquad
\hat\mu
=
\int_{\mathcal X}\hat Y\,d\nu,
\qquad
b=1,\ldots,B.
\end{gather*}\]
The observed statistic is computed analogously,
\[\begin{gather*}
\hat B_{\mathrm{samp}}^{\mathrm{obs}}
=
\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n
\hat Y(x_i)
-
\hat\mu.
\end{gather*}\]
Thus, the null distribution quantifies the sampling bias that would arise if floats followed the estimated non-preferential sampling intensity while the estimated OHC field remained fixed.
What it delivers.

Evidence for preferential sampling. A Monte Carlo \(p\)-value compares the observed statistic with the null distribution,

\[\begin{gather*}
p
=
\frac{
1+
\#\left\{
b:
\left|
\hat B_{\mathrm{samp}}^{(b)}
\right|
\ge
\left|
\hat B_{\mathrm{samp}}^{\mathrm{obs}}
\right|
\right\}
}{B+1}.
\end{gather*}\]
A small \(p\)-value indicates that the observed alignment between the sampling design and the estimated OHC field is unlikely under the non-preferential sampling model encoded by \(\lambda_0\).

A conservativeness diagnostic. The spread of \(\{\hat B_{\mathrm{samp}}^{(b)}\}\) represents the sampling bias expected under the null design. Comparing an upper null quantile, such as \(q_{0.975}\!\left(\{|\hat B_{\mathrm{samp}}^{(b)}|\}\right)\), with the deterministic Cauchy–Schwarz bound \(\sigma_w\sigma_Y\) indicates how conservative the worst-case bound is for the estimated field and sampling design. The null quantile characterizes typical bias under the assumed null model; it is not itself an upper bound on the realized preferential-sampling bias.

title: GP Modeling
text:
This report picks up where nac.qmd’s Analysis Region / OHC Anomaly sections leave off, without re-running any of that report’s plotting or re-deriving its climatology. nac.qmd publishes the ANALYSIS_BOUNDS-restricted, multi-year truth field and the truth-vs-climatology anomaly cells to data/ohc_truth/; loading those directly (~1s) skips both the expensive depth-integration step (cached separately, per year, as data/ohc_truth/truth_ohc_<year>.nc) and the RG climatology reprocessing, neither of which this report needs to repeat.


Setup — imports and experiment configuration
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath("../code"))

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import xarray as xr
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import ohc
import ohc_bias
import ohc_climatology as ohc_clim

YEARS = [2020, 2021, 2022]

ANALYSIS_BOUNDS = (36, 40, -68, -62)
EXTENT_WIDE     = [-76, -47, 28, 50]
OHC_TRUTH_DIR   = "../data/ohc_truth"
DEPTHS          = ["ohc_700", "ohc_2000"]




Load nac.qmd’s published truth field and truth-vs-climatology anomaly
truth_field_all = xr.open_dataset(f"{OHC_TRUTH_DIR}/truth_field_analysis.nc").load()
truth_anom_cells_all = pd.read_csv(f"{OHC_TRUTH_DIR}/truth_anom_cells_all.csv", parse_dates=["month"])

title: GP Modeling
text:
Background
We interpolate real Argo float OHC (ohc_700/ohc_2000 directly, not their anomaly relative to climatology) onto the full analysis-domain grid at the native 1/12\(^\circ\) resolution, using a Gaussian process fit with GpGp. Each real float profile is assigned the GLORYS truth OHC at its position and date.
code/ohc_gp_fit.R fits one GP per depth (0-700m, 0-2000m) with the stationary spatiotemporal covariance function matern_spheretime, pooling all profiles across all three years, with covariance parameters \(\theta\) = (\(\sigma^2\), \(\rho_s\), \(\rho_t\), \(\nu\), \(\tau^2\)) = (variance, spatial range, temporal range, smoothness, nugget). Smoothness is fixed at \(\nu = \tfrac{1}{2}\), where the Matérn form reduces to the simple exponential \(k(r) = e^{-r}\).
The temporal coordinate is a continuous day count (days since 2020-01-01), not day-of-year – so the fitted temporal range can extend across years rather than being capped at within-year scale by an annual wraparound, letting the model express long-range temporal autocorrelation if the data support it.
Fitting and prediction are separate scripts, so one fit is reused across both prediction grids below. code/ohc_gp_predict.R predicts via a custom Vecchia kriging step (kriging_predict) that gives both a mean and a standard error – GpGp::predictions() alone is faster but has no SE. Predicting on the coarse 1° grid (35 points) conditions each point on every one of the ~3,450 pooled profiles (“exact” mode) – cheap at that grid size, and matches the reference. Predicting on the native 1/12° grid (~3,600 points x 36 months x 2 depths) with that same full conditioning does not scale – so that pass instead uses a bounded Vecchia neighbourhood (m = 30, matching the fit’s own approximation), which reuses the same underlying machinery but conditions each point on only its 30 nearest profiles.

title: GP Modeling
text:
Model Fit


Sample GLORYS truth OHC at each real Argo profile’s position/date
_dates = xr.DataArray(pd.to_datetime(argo_a["date"]).values, dims="profile")
_lats  = xr.DataArray(argo_a["lat"].values, dims="profile")
_lons  = xr.DataArray(argo_a["lon"].values, dims="profile")
_truth_at_real = truth_field_all.interp(time=_dates, latitude=_lats, longitude=_lons, method="nearest")

real_argo_direct = argo_a[["lat", "lon", "date"]].copy()
for _d in DEPTHS:
    real_argo_direct[_d] = _truth_at_real[_d].values




Fit the pooled GP once, then predict on the 1\(^\circ\) (exact) and native 1/12\(^\circ\) (bounded Vecchia) grids
_GP_DIR = "../data/gp_interp"
os.makedirs(_GP_DIR, exist_ok=True)
_real_profiles_csv    = f"{_GP_DIR}/nac_gp_profiles_real_direct.csv"
_real_fit_summary_csv = f"{_GP_DIR}/nac_gp_real_direct_fit_summary.csv"
_real_model_cache     = f"{_GP_DIR}/nac_gp_real_direct_model_cache.rds"

_keys = ["month", "cell_lat", "cell_lon"]
_GP_MONTH_DAY = "middle"
_GP_RES_LABELS = {1 / 12: "1/12$^\circ$ (native)", 1.0: "1$^\circ$"}
# Full conditioning ("exact") is fine at 35 points; the ~3,600-point native
# grid needs the bounded Vecchia approximation to stay fast -- see Background.
_GP_PREDICT_M = {1 / 12: 30, 1.0: "exact"}
_pred_grid_csvs = {deg: f"{_GP_DIR}/nac_gp_pred_grid_{deg:.4g}deg.csv" for deg in [1 / 12, 1.0]}
_real_out_csvs  = {deg: f"{_GP_DIR}/nac_gp_real_predictions_{deg:.4g}deg.csv" for deg in [1 / 12, 1.0]}

def _grid_lats_lons(deg):
    """Prediction grid for one resolution -- native truth grid, or a deg-degree coarsening of it."""
    if deg < 0.1:
        return truth_field_all["latitude"].values, truth_field_all["longitude"].values
    _tc = ohc.coarsen_truth(truth_field_all, deg=deg)
    return np.sort(_tc["cell_lat"].unique()), np.sort(_tc["cell_lon"].unique())

for deg, grid_csv in _pred_grid_csvs.items():
    lats, lons = _grid_lats_lons(deg)
    ohc_clim.build_pred_grid(lats, lons).to_csv(grid_csv, index=False)

ohc_clim.write_profile_csv(real_argo_direct, _real_profiles_csv, suffix="")
# Fit + predict are the expensive steps (pooled GP fit, bounded-Vecchia predict
# on the ~3,600-point native grid); skip both if the fit summary and model
# cache are already on disk. Delete both files to force a re-fit/re-predict.
if not (os.path.exists(_real_fit_summary_csv) and os.path.exists(_real_model_cache)):
    ohc_clim.run_gp_fit(_real_profiles_csv, _real_fit_summary_csv, _real_model_cache)
    for deg, out_csv in _real_out_csvs.items():
        ohc_clim.run_gp_predict(
            _real_profiles_csv, _pred_grid_csvs[deg], _real_model_cache, out_csv,
            m=_GP_PREDICT_M[deg], month_day=_GP_MONTH_DAY,
        )

gp_fit_summary = ohc_clim.load_gp_fit_summary(_real_fit_summary_csv)
gp_fit_summary.groupby("depth")[["loglik", "converged", "n_obs"]].first()

Load the GP-predicted OHC field directly (no climatology to add back)
def build_gp_res_ctx(out_csv, deg, profiles_df=None):
    """Load GP predictions from out_csv and assemble the display context for resolution deg.

    Modeling OHC directly (suffix="" throughout) means the GP's prediction *is*
    the field -- no per-year climatology reassembly needed here, unlike the
    anomaly-modeling case.
    """
    lats, lons = _grid_lats_lons(deg)
    gp_field = ohc_clim.load_gp_anomaly_field(out_csv, lats, lons, suffix="")

    cells    = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(gp_field)
    se_cells = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(gp_field, value_cols=("ohc_700_se", "ohc_2000_se"))
    obs_cells = (
        ohc.grid_cells(profiles_df, list(DEPTHS), deg=1 / 12)
        if profiles_df is not None else None
    )

    if deg < 0.1:
        truth_monthly_all = truth_field_all.resample(time="1MS").mean().rename({"time": "month"})
        _bf = (gp_field[list(DEPTHS)] - truth_monthly_all[list(DEPTHS)]).rename(
            {d: f"{d}_bias" for d in DEPTHS}
        )
        bias_cells  = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(_bf, value_cols=[f"{d}_bias" for d in DEPTHS])
        truth_cells = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(truth_monthly_all)
    else:
        # coarsen_truth resamples+bins by each row's own date, so one call over
        # all three concatenated years already yields distinct per-year cells.
        truth_by_deg_all = ohc.coarsen_truth(truth_field_all, deg=deg)
        _merged = cells.merge(
            truth_by_deg_all[_keys + list(DEPTHS)].rename(columns={d: f"{d}_truth" for d in DEPTHS}),
            on=_keys, how="left",
        )
        bias_cells = _merged.copy()
        for _d in DEPTHS:
            bias_cells[f"{_d}_bias"] = bias_cells[_d] - bias_cells[f"{_d}_truth"]
        truth_cells = truth_by_deg_all

    return dict(
        cells=cells, se_cells=se_cells, obs_cells=obs_cells,
        bias_cells=bias_cells, truth_cells=truth_cells,
        obs_profiles=profiles_df,
    )

def build_gp_ctx(out_csvs, profiles_df=None):
    return {_GP_RES_LABELS[deg]: build_gp_res_ctx(out_csv, deg, profiles_df) for deg, out_csv in out_csvs.items()}

_real_ctx = build_gp_ctx(_real_out_csvs, profiles_df=real_argo_direct)




Format the pooled GP fit parameter table for display
def _fmt_fit_summary(df):
    d = df.copy()
    d["display"] = d.apply(
        lambda r: f"{r['estimate']:.4g}" if pd.isna(r["std_error"])
        else f"{r['estimate']:.4g} +/- {r['std_error']:.3g}",
        axis=1,
    )
    wide = d.pivot(index="parameter", columns="depth", values="display")
    order = ["mean (intercept)", "variance", "spatial_range", "temporal_range", "smoothness", "nugget"]
    return wide.reindex([p for p in order if p in wide.index])

EARTH_RADIUS_KM = 6371.0
_scale = {
    "mean (intercept)": ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ, "variance": ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ ** 2,
    "spatial_range": EARTH_RADIUS_KM, "temporal_range": 1.0,
    "smoothness": 1.0, "nugget": 1.0,
}
_disp = gp_fit_summary.copy()
_disp["estimate"] = _disp.apply(lambda r: r["estimate"] * _scale[r["parameter"]], axis=1)
_disp["std_error"] = _disp.apply(lambda r: r["std_error"] * _scale[r["parameter"]], axis=1)
fit_table = _fmt_fit_summary(_disp)
fit_table.index.name = "parameter"
fit_table
